- Kirk, Paul, Crime investigation, John
Wiley & Sons Canada, Limited, 1953

Ihave not reinvented the wheel on forensic chem-
istry in this book. This book is merely an effort to
consolidate previously developed, yet scattered,
forensic science related information under one um-
brella. I used all reliable resources that my prede-
cessors and contemporary experts in the field of
forensic science have developed. For this reason, I
consider myself an editor rather than an author of
this book. I have used many original illustrations in
this book. I also used some photos from the public
domain (Google) of and other (forensic-related
public domains) websites. Thank you for posting
valuable photos on your websites for educational
purposes. Primarily, this book is a milestone text-
book toward teaching forensic science at Police
Academies, Police Training colleges and universi-
ties.

- TARUVU SURESH ACKNOWLEDGMENTS

1 FORENSIC SCIENCE

Introduction Forensic Science plays a pivotal role in Criminal Justice system just as Science
and Technology plays an important role in enriching modern society in all walks
of life. Forensic science is a dynamic discipline. Today, Forensic Science is facing
many challenges that stretch its resources to their limits. There are new chal-
lenges and new type of crimes are emerging, so the way evidence is created
and captured is changing as are the public expectations of their police and the
criminal justice system. The application of latest scientific tools and techniques
for proving the guilt or innocence of the accused by the courts of law is broadly
known as Forensic Science. The term forensic is derived from the Latin word
“Forensis” which means “belonging to the Court of justice”. Forensic Science
can be defined as “the science which deals with the recognition, identification,
individualization and evaluation of physical evidence by the application of prin- individualization and evaluation of physical evidence by the application of prin-
ciples and the methods of natural sciences in the administration of justice”. In Forensic Science, the laws of various facets of science are applied in con-
ducting an analysis to determine the nature and characteristics of physical evi-
dence collected in the process of crime or civil investigation. Using the scientific
methods inferences are drawn about, how the evidence can be linked to crime
and criminal. ‘These inferences are thus connected to the events that may or may
not have taken place in connection with physical evidence. It is valid to opine

that the law defines elements of a crime whereas the science contributes infor-

mation which assists in determining whether an element is present or absent.

Scope mation which assists in determining whether an element is present or absent.

Scope

Forensic science has scope in standardization and guidance in the field of
Forensics. This includes the development of standards that pertain to laboratory
and field based forensic science techniques and methodology in general areas
such as the detection and collection of physical evidence, the subsequent analysis,
interpretation of the evidence, reporting of results and findings. Forensic analysis
is performed on physical evidence to scientifically assist the police and also the FORENSIC SCIENCE | 4 court in establishing physical facts so that criminal or civil disputes can be
resolved. It is the prime motive of the forensic scientist to translate the legal
inquiry into appropriate scientific questions and to advice the Investigating
Officer or the judiciary on the capabilities, limitations and results of the analytical
techniques. Forensic science has vast scope in government sectors and law
enforcement agencies like police, legal system and investigative services of the
central and state governments. Private agencies also hire people with qualification
in this field. A major lot of forensic scientists are hired by the Intelligence
Bureau, the CBI etc. Another important option for forensic scientists is teaching
forensic science in Universities and also Police Academies. They can start career
as a Forensic Expert, Forensic Scientist, Forensic Investigator, Crime Scene
Investigator, Handwriting Expert, Document Expert, Document Examiner, as a Forensic Expert, Forensic Scientist, Forensic Investigator, Crime Scene
Investigator, Handwriting Expert, Document Expert, Document Examiner,
Fraud Examiner, Forensic Analyst, Scientific Officer, Security Expert, Forensic
Consultant, Detective, Investigating Officer, Teacher/Professor, Crime Reporter,
Forensic Engineer, Forensic Psychologist, Fingerprint Expert, Cyber Crime
Expert, Ethical Hacker, Forensic Mobile Expert, CDR Expert, Law Consultant,
Government Examiner, Instructor, Genetics Experts, Environment Analyst,
Technicians, Forensic Medical Examiners, Technician etc. Governing Principles and Laws of Forensic Science

There are 7 basic principles in Forensic Sciences.

1. Law of Comparison. 2. Law of Individuality.
3. Law of Analysis. 4. Law of Probability.

5. Law of Progressive Change. 6. Principle of Exchange.
7. Law of Circumstantial Facts.

1. Law of Comparison

The law of comparison is “Compare like with like”. It emphasizes the necessity
of providing similar samples and specimens for comparison.
No purpose will be served if comparison is made between

e Black Fiber vs Black hair e Telugu Writing vs English Writing
e Grey Hair vs Grey Fiber e SBML vs SBBL
e Bullet .38 vs Bullet .32 e DBBLvs Rifle

e Nylon Fiber vs Cotton Fiber

FORENSIC SCIENCE | 5

3. Law of Analysis

The principle of Law of Analysis emphasizes the necessity of correct sampling
and packaging for effective use of experts. Hence proper collection of sample
and also its integrity and chain of custody is important. “Conclusion or
interpretation without analysis can be no better than the sample analysed”.

For example, blood drops found in the two sites at the same scene may not
belong to one person. FORENSIC SCIENCE | 6

4. Law of Probability

Probability is defined as the frequency of occurrence of an event. In flipping a
coin, probability is easy to establish. It determines the chances of occurrence or
non occurrence of a particular event, in a particular way with equal probability.

For example, the exact probability of some things is almost impossible to
define- like transferring dog hair or blue jeans fiber. The probability of stating
the common origin of the specimens is not possible for all the cases. All definite
or indefinite identifications made consciously or unconsciously are on the basis
of law of probability.

5. Law of Progressive Change

“Everything in the Universe undergoes a change as the time progresses.” Its
impact on forensic science is immense. The criminal, the crime scene and
objects involved in the crime all of them undergo changes, hence may become
unrecognizable.

For example:

People may undergo change-

e Criminals - Disguise, Alibi For example:

People may undergo change-

e Criminals - Disguise, Alibi

e Witnesses - may turn Hostile, be influenced

e Police - may be on transfer, promotion, retirement

e Judges - may be on transfer, promotion, retirement

e Prosecutors - may be on transfer, promotion, retirement
e Lawyers - may be busy with new cases, priorities

Places may undergo change-

e Crime scene — is disturbed by persons, animals, contamination, weather,
atmosphere, rain, sun, birds, maggots

e Objects/materials/matter — may become volatile, decomposed,
chemically, Physically, Biologically transformed.

Hence speedy investigation, trial and justice are important. “As the justice
delayed is justice denied”.

6. Principle of Exchange

A French scientist Prof. Edmond Locard in 1928 proposed the theory of
principle of exchange. This was the first and foremost concept that led to the
systematic development of forensic science.

FORENSIC SCIENCE | 7

According to this, “Whenever any two objects come into contact with one
another there is always an exchange of material”. When a criminal or crime
weapon comes in contact with the victim or the objects surrounding him, they
leave traces, in the same way the criminal or crime weapon picks up traces from
the same contact. It is the responsibility of the investigating officer to search,
locate and collect the physical evidence.

7. Law of Circumstantial Facts

“Facts do not lie, men can and do,” hence the importance of circumstantial
evidence is as good as oral evidence. Oral evidence depends upon so many
factors such as power of observation, assimilation, and auto suggestion.

History, Growth and Development of Forensic Science
in Crime Investigation

The history of Forensic science i.e. applying "scientific" principles to legal
questions has a long and intriguing history. Notable contributions are made by
the following eminent scientists and scholars: Famous People in History of Forensic Science:

Alphonse Bertillon

Bertillon was a French police
officer and biometrics
researcher who applied the
anthropological technique of
anthropometry to law
enforcement creating an
identification system based
on physical measurements.
In 1879 he developed
‘Anthropometry’ -a
systematic procedure for :
describing the Body Measurements. In 1881 he took standard pictures of all
French criminals and developed a technique called Portrait Parle— a method
of identifying culprits from description provided by victim and witness. He
is also the inventor of the mug shot. FORENSIC SCIENCE | 8

Mathieu Orfila

Mathieu Orfila was a towering figure in the
emergent field of Forensics. His research
contribution was mostly on Toxicology. He
also published a book on poisons in 1814
which earned him the title of “Father of
Toxicology". He was the first great
exponent of Forensic Medicine in 19th-
century.

Growth and Development of Forensic Science Laboratories

1.

9.

First Forensic Science Laboratory was established in 1923 at Los Angles -
USA.

Metropolitan Forensic Science Laboratory was established in 1935 at
Scotland Yard - UK.

After World War II there was sudden increase in crime rate due to
availability of firearms in large scale. Today there are about 1100 Forensic
Science Laboratories in 89 countries across the world.

In India Chemical Examiners Laboratories were set up by British at Madras,
Calcutta, Agra and Bombay in second half of 19th century. In India Chemical Examiners Laboratories were set up by British at Madras,
Calcutta, Agra and Bombay in second half of 19th century.

The Finger Print Bureau of the world was established in1897 at Calcutta.
By 1910 most of the states established Finger Print Bureaux.

GEQD (Government Examiner for Questioned Documents) —was
established at Simla in 1906.

Serologist and Chemical Examiner for Government of India was
established in 1910 at Calcutta. Scientific sections under State CID‘s have
started examination of fire arms, footprints, documents, photography, etc.;
during this period.

First state Forensic Science Laboratory was established in 1952 at Calcutta.

First Central Forensic Science Laboratory was established in 1957 at
Calcutta.

Andhra Pradesh Forensic Science Laboratory (combined) was established
in November, 1974.

Separate Andhra Pradesh and Telangana Forensic Science Laboratories are
established after bifurcation of two Telugu states in 2019.

Forensic Science Laboratory and its structure

1.

The Government of Andhra Pradesh established a full-fledged Forensic
Science Laboratory in the year 1974. The Laboratory was headed by a Di-
rector who reports to the Director General of Police. Four Joint Directors support the Director and are in-charge of Biological,
Chemical, Physical and General Divisions. Under each division there are
different sections, in total 13 sections. They are Forensic Chemistry, Foren-
sic Toxicology, Forensic Narcotics, Forensic Serology, Forensic Biology,
DNA Finger Printing, Forensic Bio-Medical, Forensic Physics, Forensic
Ballistics, Forensic Engineering, Forensic Computers, Forensic Documents,
and Forensic Psychology.

Each section is headed by an Assistant Director with supporting scientific
staff in the cadre of Scientific Officers, Scientific Assistants, Lab Assistants,
Lab Technicians and Lab Attendants.

Divisions and Sections in Forensic Science Laboratory are as follows:

Chemical Division Biological Divisions | Physical Divisions General Divisions
1. Forensic Chemistry| 1. Forensic Serology 1. Forensic Physics 1. Forensic Computers
2. Forensic Toxicology) 2. Forensic Biology 2. Forensic Ballistics 2. Forensic Documents 3. Forensic Narcotics | 3. DNA Finger Printing | 3. Forensic Engineering | 3. Forensic Psychology

4, Forensic Bio-Medical

Eight Regional Forensic Science Laboratories were there in undivided state
of Andhra Pradesh. After bifurcation of the state in residual AP five Re-
gional Forensic Science Laboratories has been functioning at Visakhapat-
nam, Vijayawada, Guntur, Tirupati and Kurnool. Later two more RFSL’s
were sanctioned by the government at Ananthapur and Rajamahendravaram.
In Telangana Warangal, Kamareddy and Karimnagar. The Regional labora- FORENSIC SCIENCE | 12

tories are headed by Assistant Directors as in- charge officers with other

Forensic Expert Evidence in Court of Law

Expert testimony plays an
important role in forensic
science. An expert’s scientific
findings, no matter how
important, have no_ real
meaning in law until they are
presented to the judge or jury.
The expert evidence is
covered mainly under the
section 45 of the Indian
Evidence Act, 1872 and
section 293 of The Criminal Procedure Code, 1974. Section 45 of IEA states that: Opinions of experts - When the Court has
to form an opinion upon a point of foreign law, or of science, or art, or as to
identity of hand writing or finger impressions, the opinions upon that point

FORENSIC SCIENCE | 19

of persons specially skilled in such foreign law, science or art, or in questions
as to identity of handwriting or finger impressions, are relevant facts. Such
persons called experts. The expert performs the required analysis, makes ob-
servations, collect the necessary data and draw certain conclusions based on
the analysis. The presentation is usually in the form of a written report.

Importance of Expert Witnesses

Their services are utilized by law

1. In cases involving the negligence (malpractice) of a Professional.

2. In cases concerning complex technological issues where the subject is suf-
ficiently beyond common experience.

3. To assist the courts, where the opinion of an expert may be of some assis-
tance to the judges and lawyers.

Impact of Expert Witnesses

1. The expert explains the logic mechanism involved, be it a technique, me-
chanical or medical procedure.

2. The expert gives an authoritative opinion of causation and fault. Report: An expert’s report is issued on a standard pattern. The report should
be concise and intelligible to prove the conclusions arrived at, convincingly. As
the reports are to be utilized by the non-technical laymen in most of the cases,
in the absence of experts, under section 293 of the Code of Criminal
Procedure, 1974, therefore are expressed in simple language. The wording of
the inference should be standardized to increase clarity and obviate chances of
misinterpretation. The reports should be illustrated with the experimental data,
photographs, illustrations and with sketches whenever possible and necessary.
The conclusions which form the most important part of the report can either
be definite or indefinite based only on the probability figures. However the
inconclusive reports are probed to ascertain their probative values and are given
due weight-age accordingly. Expert evidence is verifiable and leaves no room
for bias and can always be sent for second opinion. Admissibility of Evidence

Admission of evidence requires a foundation. Through the use of witness’s
testimony, the validity of the physical evidence can be established. The typical
process involves the labeling of an item as an exhibit and foundation being laid FORENSIC SCIENCE | 20

by the expert witness through a process of verification so that the exhibit can
be accepted as evidence in court of law.

Court: The court is the ultimate authority to evaluate and utilize the
expert evidence. The scrutiny of expert evidence by the court is necessary
in the dissemination of justice. The scientific methods in the detection of
the crime are daily improving and are becoming accurate, sensitive and
specific. It should be made sure that the expert evidence produced is
relevant, intelligible, properly demonstrated and explained and may conform
to the standards in the specialty.

GRIME SCENE MANAGEMENT

Introduction

Most of the Police investigations begin at the scene of a crime. The scene is
defined as ‘the actual site or location or where the incident takes place’. It is
important that the first officer on the crime scene properly protects the
evidence. The entire investigation hinges on that first person being able to
properly identify, isolate, and secure the scene. Contemporary law enforcement
has greatly expanded its ability to solve crimes by the adoption of forensic
techniques and procedures. Today, crimes often can be solved by detailed
examination of the crime scene and analysis of forensic evidence. The work of forensic scientists is not only crucial in criminal investigations
and prosecutions, but is also vital in civil litigations, major man-made and
natural disasters, and the investigation of global crimes. The success of the
analysis of the forensic evidence is based upon a system that emphasizes
teamwork, advanced investigative skills and tools (such as GPS positioning, ce
phone tracking, video image analysis, artificial intelligence and data mining),
and the ability to process a crime scene properly by recognizing, collecting and
preserving all relevant physical evidence. After physical evidence has been
identified at a scene, subsequent preservation, handling and analysis must satisfy
both scientific and legal requirements if that evidence is to be considered
reliable and useful in court proceedings. Recognition of physical evidence is a vital step in the process. If potential
physical evidence is not recognized, collected or properly preserved and
tested, the forensic value of the evidence may be greatly reduced or even
lost forever. Numerous routine and high profile cases have demonstrated
the harsh reality that despite the availability of current crime scene
technologies, specialized equipment, and sophisticated forensic laboratory
analysis, the effective utilization of physical evidence in crime solving is only
as good as the knowledge and integrity of the crime scene personnel and
the objective legal system that supports those functions. In some cases,
evidence has been falsified or results tainted, misleading the justice system. CRIME SCENE MANAGEMENT | 24

Types of Crime Scenes

It is essential that crime scene investigators develop the ability to utilize their
analytical skills and logical approaches to make an initial determination
regarding the number and types of crime scenes that were involved in the
commission of the crime. Finding the exact, precise boundaries of a crime
scene may be difficult, and may require adjustments after the initial
observations.

Once a crime scene has been defined and a “boundary” has been established,
the crime scene(s) should be secured and processed accordingly. Any actions
taken at the crime scene should meet all the legal and scientific standards and
requirements. Once the locations are determined, the proper sequence of
events of the crime can be established, and thus the physical evidence from
each of the scenes can be successfully recognized.

Crime Scene Processing

Scene of Crime: The scene of crime cannot be limited to one place only. For
example in a scene of crime such as burglary it may be divided into the
following:

1. Line of approach
2. Point of entry

3. Actual scene

4. Point of exit and
5. Line of retreat 1. Line of approach
2. Point of entry

3. Actual scene

4. Point of exit and
5. Line of retreat

The importance of the crime scene, its protection and preservation cannot be
over-emphasized. Successful investigation of a crime largely depends upon how
well various clues, leads, and evidence available at the scene of crime are located,
protected, recorded, processed, evaluated and interpreted. The observations
and findings in an undisturbed crime scene are vital to the success of the
investigation. An improperly secured and unprotected crime scene may lead to
loss, destruction or contamination of various forms of evidence, especially
physical evidence. Moreover evidence once overlooked cannot be retrieved at
a later stage. Therefore the scene of crime should be properly secured and
protected as a first step by the investigator and all available evidence preserved
in an appropriate manner thereafter.

Protection and Isolation of Crime Scene

The first action of the investigating officer who first reaches the scene should Protection and Isolation of Crime Scene

The first action of the investigating officer who first reaches the scene should

be, to secure the crime scene from unauthorized persons by setting up
barricades or crime scene protection tapes and cordoning off the required area.
The best physical evidence is normally found at the place where use of force
against persons or property has taken place. Sometimes valuable pieces of
evidence may be discarded or dropped or it may fall off accidentally or
inadvertently at some distance from the scene of crime. Such spots also need
proper protection. All such critical areas should be secured from intruders and
stray animals or neighbors, friends, sympathizers, curious onlookers, newspaper
reporters, press photographers and others who are not officially connected.
The whole area should be cleared of all unauthorized persons in quickest
possible time. The Press and other Media should be requested to keep beyond
the barricaded/secured area. The following steps should be taken for protection of the scene of the crime

1.

6. The first priority is to extend medical aid to the injured persons so as to
save their lives.
Cordon off the scene and its surroundings, at entry and exit points
effectively.
Identify and persuade to retain the person who first informed the Police
or else note his address, telephone number etc, for further contacts.
Persons who ate likely to provide information, investigative leads and other
pertinent details should be segregated and examined for collecting all
possible information. Eye-witnesses should be requested and allowed to
stay at earmarked place for examination.
Physical evidence should be protected from pet animals like dogs, cats,
rabbits, rats, mice, birds, and also some adverse environmental influences
such as wind, rain, sunlight, dust, smoke, moisture, etc.
Physical evidence should not be disturbed from its original position without
proper recording, Jewelry, keys, currency notes and other valuable items Physical evidence should not be disturbed from its original position without
proper recording, Jewelry, keys, currency notes and other valuable items
which are of evidentiary value may be vulnerable to theft from the scene.
Preservation of these items have to do done immediately after a seizure list
is drafted. During the preliminary inspection of the crime scene care should be taken
to ensure that nothing is inadvertently dropped or added such as cigarette
butts, match boxes, match sticks, empty packs of cigarettes, ash, hair, etc.
even ensure that nothing is touched that may cause interference with the
existing fingerprints, footprints, etc. CRIME SCENE MANAGEMENT | 28

8. All physical/traces evidence should be lifted only after recording and
thoroughly searching the crime scene. Where traffic is to be restored or
place is required for use, examination of scene and other work should be
completed as expeditiously as possible but with thoroughness.

It should be borne in mind that evidence may not always be visible to eye. Even
if visible, it may escape a non-observant eye or may not appear relevant. It is
therefore necessary that the scene or places be subjected to close scrutiny by a
team of Forensic experts (Clues Team) and Investigating personnel.

Preservation of Physical evidence Preservation of Physical evidence

Certain types of evidence are likely to get evaporated, decomposed, putrefied,
degenerated by environmental factors or may undergo other chemical or
biological changes with time. Therefore for packing suitable containers like
polythene or glass bottles, polythene bags, paper envelopes, aluminum foils,
Faraday bags etc., are to be used to preserve their evidentiary value.

Recording and Documentation Methods of the Crime Scene Recording and Documentation Methods of the Crime Scene

The police have limited time and opportunity to make an in-depth study of the
crime scene in an undisturbed state. No time should therefore be lost to record
as accurately as possible the conditions existing when investigating officer
arrives at the scene. Such a record will help the on-going investigations,
reconstruction of the scene of crime and also presentation of the same in the
Court. The judiciary and defense counsel need convincing and trust worthy
evidences to substantiate the conditions and circumstances reported prevailing
at the time of the crime. It is therefore necessary to make a visual presentation
that shows the various evidentiary items, witnesses and their inter relationship
with the scene of crime. The methods of crime scene recording are
Photography, Sketch, Observation notes, Videography and Audio tape
recording.

Photography is a useful method to make a permanent record of the crime scene
and facilitates the reconstruction of the crime scene and description of the
method by which the crime was committed.

Guidelines for crime scene photography
1.

The scene of crime should be kept undisturbed before taking thepho-
tographs.

The working condition of camera, lens, flash, batteries, memory card/film,
etc. should be checked before attempting any photograph. The film or the
other recording medium such as tape, memory card, CD-R etc. used should
be appropriate for the lighting conditions and capabilities of the camera.

Before commencing the work, the whole area should be inspected and all
the shots required should be carefully planned.

While planning the number of shots ensures that, enough overlapping shots
are included to sequentialise the scene.

Initially sufficient number of shots should be taken to give an overall per-
spective of the place of occurrence, approach to the place, entry, access
and exit points. While photographing indoor or outdoor evidentiary items, enough over-
lapping shots and close-up shots should be taken to bring out interrelation-
ships and also to capture minute details. It is better to have three views
taken viz.; overall view, mid-range view, and close-up view of all important
items of evidence.

All photographs should be taken at eye level. In some unusual cases aerial
views or below normal views may be taken depending on the crime scene
and surroundings.

The chance fingerprints, footprints, etc. left at the scene of crime, should
always be exposed from a close distance.

It is mandatory to place a scale or foot rule beside evidentiary items such
as footprints, knife, injuries, cut marks, etc. to indicate the actual size in the
enlarged photographs.

. It is worthwhile to keep separate numbered cards or plates for each evi-

dentiary item so as to ensure proper identification/sequence at a later stage.

it should be properly labeled, with details of the place, event, police station,

Name of the photographer and address.

Photography log with description.

Direction and distance to be maintained between the lens and the object.

Technical information such as Type of Camera, Focal length of lens, Speed
of the lens, Effective aperture, Shutter speed, Film speed, Type of Flash ete.

Similar information should also be maintained to digital still or video cameras.

Special techniques such as oblique light, flood lamps, flashlight, UV light,
oli-light, Filters etc., if used, should be noted against each shot.

Light and weather conditions in cases of outdoor shots.

Videography

Videography is useful in making a record of a crime scene, which help in scene
reconstruction of crime scene at a later stage. In certain crimes like arson,
explosion Videography provides an overall view of the scene and allows
investigators who could not be present at the scene to visualize the important
pieces of evidence and their relationship with one another.

Guidelines for Crime Scene Videography:

1.

Videotaping should be used to supplement the information that is collected
from other recording procedures viz., photography, digital photography,
sketching and observation notes. A videotape review conducted with all the connected personnel will help
reconstruction of the crime scene. It is advantageous because of its freeze
frame capability, which allows Investigating Officers to replay, study and
discuss particular scenes as often as necessary for reconstruction and
continue with the investigation.
It is also possible to locate certain minute pieces of evidence recorded
immediately after the incident but overlooked for collection.
The process of video documentation is similar to still photography. For
example in a burglary case the Videography should start at the
actual/suspected point of entry, cover the crime scene, point of exit and
line of retreat. All-important spots can be focused through a zoom lens
and more time can be allotted to cover from all angles. CRIME SCENE MANAGEMENT | 32

5. The voice recording capability can be used to describe what is seen while
recording simultaneously. This capability can also be used while presenting
to the Court to serve as a convincing proof.

Crime Scene Sketch

After the scene is photographed, it should be sketched, to provide
measurements indicating the relative distance and position of the various items
of evidence and their interrelationships. The sketch is the first visual record of the condition of the crime scene. It
portrays the crime scene and items within that scene that are of interest to the
investigation. Sketches properly prepared are useful during the interrogation
of witnesses, in making notes in the case diaries and in presenting information
to the court. The sketch complements the photographs and notes made while
observing the crime scene. The ultimate purpose of the crime scene sketch
drawn as per set scale and plan, which is also known as crime scene planning.
It represents the facts of the crime with such clarity and precision that the crime
itself may be reconstructed from the details. Courts rely to a great extent on
sketch of the scene prepared.

1. The orientation of the scene with reference to “magnetic north” is manda-
tory to be mentioned at the top of the sketch. 1. The orientation of the scene with reference to “magnetic north” is manda-
tory to be mentioned at the top of the sketch.

2. In indoor crime scenes the size of the window(s) and room(s) and in out-

like trees, electric or telephone poles, lamp posts or other landmarks should
be indicated.

3. The approach to the crime scene such as roadways, streets, by lanes, path-
ways, etc. should be drawn to facilitate reconstruction.

4. The position of evidences in the scene like blood stains, footprints, tyre or
drag marks and any other objects are to be indicated with accurate meas-
urements from at least two fixed points.

5. The following information must invariably be mentioned in the sketch.

e Crime number, section of law, police station, and district

Crime Scene Observation Notes

Investigating officer should note the following observations in crime scene:

1. Method of Recording: The time of receipt of first information to be
noted first. The notes can be recorded as and when the observations are
made and need not be in a sequence. CRIME SCENE MANAGEMENT | 38

6.

Voice Recording

Date, time and location: The time and date of arrival at crime scene and
the details of its location, environmental conditions (smoke, rain, fog, dust,
etc.), names of persons present at the scene on arrival, nature of odors if
any etc, should be recorded.

Description of the victim and clothing: The name, age, height, weight,
complexion, colour of hair and eyes and, if possible, the date of birth of
the victim should be noted. Outer garments should be described in terms
of type, texture and colour.

Wounds on the victim: The exact location of a wound or injury, its size
and colour in the case of a bruise, to be clearly mentioned.

General Description: Any minor/major damage to the items and any ap-
parent disturbance of the normal arrangement of furniture or other objects,
presence of any object that seems to be unusual in the context of the scene
should be mentioned. Recognition of each significant item of evidence: Describe of each
significant piece of evidence found or identified with special techniques
with complete details, such as the time of when and where it was found,
who found it, exact location and how it was marked, sealed and packed.

Missing items: The purpose of the entry of missing items in the obser-
vation note is to identify the items that should have been normally associ-
ated with the crime under investigation. For example, an item of clothing
missing from the deceased or victim’s body that could not be located at the
scene of crime and which, if found later from the suspect or elsewhere,
should be useful in linking evidence and suspect or vice-versa.

Other important information: The condition of doors, windows, locks,
wrist watches, wall clocks, water taps, electric lights, and other household
gadgets etc,. to be recorded carefully. Miscellaneous: Any other details not included in photographs and
sketches such as condition of surroundings, minute details on various items
of evidence such as serial number, model, make of firearm, etc., should
also be recorded. The investigating officer can reorganize all these notes
for preparing his case diary and charge sheet.

Like photography, Videography and Voice recordings are useful in making a
record of a crime scene, which may help in scene reconstruction at a later stage.
Voice recording is useful in making a record of observations of a crime scene
or statements of an eye witness which may help at later stage in crime scene

reconstruction. In certain crimes like arson, explosion, hit and run accident
scenes, Voice recordings provides an overall view of the scene and allows
investigators who could not be present at the scene to visualize the important
pieces of evidence and their relationship to one another.

Crime Scene Search methods

Crime Scene search for the Physical Evidences Crime Scene Search methods

Crime Scene search for the Physical Evidences

The crime scene is highly dynamic and is constantly undergoing change. It is
so fragile in the sense that the evidentiary value of the items can easily be lost
or downgraded. Usually there is only one opportunity to search the scene
properly. Hence making a good overall survey of the scene is mandatory and
quite essential. The primary purpose of inspecting the scene of crime is to
search for any evidence left behind by the criminal while committing the crime.
Such evidence will be invariably present in all scenes of crime and is based on
the Locard’s principle of exchange. Such exchanges of materials may be large
or small, visible or invisible, readily detectable or difficult to detect, but such
exchanges does occur. It is therefore the primary responsibility of Investigating
Officers to search, identify and collect such evidences independently or with
the assistance of ‘Clues’ Team. \ competent search of a crime scene requires a thorough understanding of
crime, its nature, extent of damage, aggrieved people, property lost or damaged
and the manner in which the offence was committed. The search should be
carried out carefully, avoiding unnecessary disturbance of the scene with equal
care and concentration.

The scene of crime can be classified as Indoor scene and outdoor scene. In
indoor crime scenes the Investigating Officer should take precautions of not
ignoring spaces which are concealed behind the wall hangings, doors, curtains,
under the carpets or mattresses, under or behind suitcases, boxes, almirahs, sofa
sets, tables, chairs, light fixtures, switch boards, fuse boxes, cracks in the floor,
new paint, plaster etc.

Methods of searching outdoor and indoor scenes of crime:
a. Zone method Methods of searching outdoor and indoor scenes of crime:
a. Zone method

If the crime scene is large and enough personnel are not available, then it is
advisable to divide or sub divide, the area into smaller zones and sub zones and
use this method of search. This method is particularly useful in detailed and CRIME SCENE MANAGEMENT | 40

careful searching in the enclosed areas for items like almirahs, safes, etc.
including search in cars or carriages which may contain minute trace evidences,
necessitating a thorough search of all nooks and corners.

b. Strip method

This is a sector search, useful for both indoor and outdoor scenes, where crime
scene is divided into segments and strips. The method works well if the area to
be searched is square or rectangular in shape with clearly marked boundaries.
The search may be conducted by one or more officers depending on the size
of the area and availability of officers.

CRIME SCENE MANAGEMENT | 41

c. Grid method

Grid method is a slight modification of the strip method. This method is useful
for small areas especially indoors. The area is divided into grids. In this method
a single person can search the entire area by moving at right angles, and covering
all the squares of the grid.

d. Spiral method

The search is conducted by following a spiral pattern working from outside to
inside towards the centre or focal point (towards the body, safe or any other
key feature) or vice versa, either clockwise or anti clockwise. It is an effective CRIME SCENE MANAGEMENT | 42

search pattern for single person search, with a very high probability of locating
minute evidence like fingerprints, footprints, etc. There is not much likelihood
of the evidence being destroyed by the investigator’s movements.

e. Radial method

n this method, the investigator works outward from the centre along the radial,
and moves in circles of increasing circumference repeatedly. This method
provides a double check on the searched area and is one of the best methods

See teeoe?™

available for both indoor and outdoor searches. This method is preferred if
more than one officer is conducting the search.

Crime Scene Reconstruction Crime Scene Reconstruction

Reconstruction involves scientific scene analysis, interpretation of the scene
pattern evidence, laboratory examination of physical evidence, along with
systematic study of related information and the logical formulation of a theory.
Reconstruction of crime or crime scene reconstruction is the forensic science
discipline in which one gains "explicit knowledge of the series of events that
surround the commission of a crime using deductive and inductive reasoning,
scientific methods, and interrelationship of physical evidence. Crime scene
reconstruction is the process of determining or eliminating the events and
actions that occurred at the crime scene through analysis of the crime scene
pattern, location and position of the physical evidence, and the laboratory
examination of the physical evidence.

CRIME SCENE MANAGEMENT | 43

Importance of Crime Scene Reconstruction

Crime scene reconstruction "involves evaluating the context of a scene and the
physical evidence found there in an effort to identify what occurred and in what
order it occurred. Crime reconstruction is the development of actions and
circumstances based on the system of evidence discovered and examined in
relation to a particular crime. It is often useful to determine the actual course of a crime by limiting the
possibilities that resulted in the crime scene or the physical evidence as
encountered. The possible need to reconstruct the crime is one major reason
for maintaining the integrity of a crime scene. Reconstruction in general refers
to having the victim, suspect, witness or other individual re-enact the event that
produced the crime scene or the physical evidence based on their knowledge
of the crime. Therefore Reconstruction is based on the ability to make
observations at the scene, the scientific ability to examine physical evidence,
and the use of logical approaches to theory formulations. CRIME SCENE MANAGEMENT | 44

Nature of Reconstruction
In forensic science, there are three important areas of determining the
components of a crime scene:
1. Specific incident reconstruction
Eg: Road traffic accidents, bombings, homicides, and accidents of any
severity.
2. Event reconstruction
Eg: Connection between evidences and sequence of events.
3. Physical evidence reconstruction

Eg: Firearms, blood traces, glass fragments and any other objects that can
be sent for DNA analysis.

Reconstruction is based on the results of crime scene examination, laboratory
analysis, and other independent sources of information of case events.
Reconstruction often involves the use of inductive and deductive logic,
statistical data, and information from the crime scene, pattern analysis, and
laboratory analytical results of various physical evidence. Reconstruction can be a very complex task, linking many types of physical
evidence, stain pattern information, analytical results, investigative information,
and other documentary and testimonial evidence into a complete entity.

CRIME SCENE MANAGEMENT | 45

There are many types of reconstruction depending on the nature of the crime,
inference drawn by questions and answers, the types of events that have taken

expert in the crime scene.

Pattern Evidence in Reconstruction Pattern evidence is one type of physical evidence encountered at a majority of
crime scenes. Pattern evidence at crime scenes is however extremely valuable
in the reconstruction of crimes and the activity that has taken place at the crime
scene. It can be used to prove or disprove a suspect’s alibi or a witness’s version
of what took place at the crime scene, to associate or dissociate the involvement
of persons or provide objects in particular events, or to provide the
investigators with new leads or information for further investigation. Pattern
evidence is generally created by contact of two surfaces (persons, vehicles, or
objects) that results in the formation of impressions, imprints or markings.
These impressions may be from static or stationary contact or dynamic or
moving contact or may be two-dimensional or three-dimensional. In some
cases, the contact may be a transfer of material from one surface to another moving contact or may be two-dimensional or three-dimensional. In some
cases, the contact may be a transfer of material from one surface to another
resulting in pattern evidence in the form of a stain or deposit. Pattern evidence
also results from the fracture, breaking or cutting of an object. The following is a list of pattern evidence commonly found at different
crime scenes

e Bloodstain patterns

e Glass fracture patterns

e Fire burn patterns

e Furniture position patterns

e =Track-trail patterns

e Modus operandi and crime scene profile patterns

Pattern evidence found at crime scenes should be carefully documented,
processed, enhanced, or collected using scientific techniques.

Bloodstain Pattern Analysis for Reconstruction

Reconstruction from bloodstain patterns has long been a neglected area, but it
has received more attention recently from the law enforcement and legal
communities. It is the evaluation of the static aftermath of bloodshed in an
attempt to determine the actions that created the bloodstains found at the crime
scene. The totality of these definitions is that the size, shape, and distribution
patterns of the bloodstains found at a crime scene where bloodshed has

50° 30° 20° 10° 50° 30° 20° 10°

occurred can be used to reconstruct the bloodshed event(s).
Reconstruction of crime scene with bloodstain evidence by observing:
1. Direction of travel of the blood droplets.

2. Distance of blood source to target surface.

3. Angle of impact of blood droplet.

4. Type of blood droplets. CRIME SCENE MANAGEMENT | 48

5. Determination of blood trails, their direction, and the relative speed of motion.

6. Nature of the force used to create the bloodshed.

7. Nature of the object used to cause bloodshed, the number of blows
involved, and relative location of persons/objects near bloodshed.

8. Sequencing of multiple events associated with the bloodshed.

9. Interpretation of contact or transfer patterns.

10. Estimation of elapsed time and volume of bloodshed.

Glass Fracture Patterns

Broken glass at crime scenes can sometimes aid in reconstruction providing
information about the events which took place, and may assist in proving or
disproving an alibi or witness’s story. Glass fracture patterns are most often
associated with burglary, criminal mischief, shooting incidents, fire scenes etc.

The most common types of information that can be obtained by
studying glass fracture patterns are

1. Direction of impact force applied (from inside out or outside in). 1. Direction of impact force applied (from inside out or outside in).

2. Approximate force of impact.

3. Approximate angle of impact of force.

4. Determination of the type of glass fracture.

5. Determination of the sequence of firing, direction of firing, and the type

of firearm for the projectile holes present.
6. Estimation of the fire temperatures, direction of fire travel, and the

intensity of heat from the melted glass.
The use of glass fracture patterns in crime scene reconstruction relies on careful
recognition, documentation, along with study of radial and concentric glass fracture
markings. Other information for reconstruction is obtained by analysis of rib marks,
spatial relationships, crack marks, and the condition of melted glass if any. If broken glass remains in the frame the observed fracture pattern will consist
of a center point, where the force contacted the windowpane, with radiating
(radial) fracture lines going out from the center point. It is possible to reassemble
the diced window and determine the location at which the projectile struck the
window, the direction of the projectile flight, an approximate angle of incident,
and in some cases whether or not more than one projectile struck the window.
This type of reconstruction is very helpful in shooting reconstruction cases

CRIME SCENE MANAGEMENT | 49

where one or more side windows have been broken by bullets.

Furniture Position Patterns

At an indoor crime scene, the position and condition of furniture often yields
information about the events that caused the pattern, their sequence, and
possible actions of perpetrators and victims. Displaced or broken furniture can
indicate that a struggle took place. Patterns of disarray in ordinary or expected
furniture placement or condition may further reveal actions taken by suspects
or witnesses.

Track-Trail Patterns Track-Trail Patterns

Occasionally track-trail patterns are encountered at crime scenes. Proper
interpretation of them can yield information about how many persons were
present at a scene, where they were moving, the nature of the movement
(walking, running) and the direction of travel, and whether heavy objects were
being carried or dragged. Some of these types of patterns can give class
characteristic information or data about the individual responsible for
producing the patterns, such as shoe size, stride length, sex, weight, or any
abnormalities in movement or gait.

Tire and Skid Mark Patterns Tire and Skid Mark Patterns

The value of skid mark patterns in traffic accident reconstructions is well
known and documented. However, the use of these patterns in crime scene
investigation and reconstruction is often neglected. Tire or skid mark patterns
are often seen at outdoor crime scenes, and can provide important
reconstruction information for the crime scene investigator. These markings
can yield information about the number and types of vehicles involved, the
possible speed of travel, direction of travel, whether or not brakes were applied,
whether turns were made or not etc.

Fire Burn Patterns

Fire burn patterns often provide information on the various factors which led
to or caused a fire. Detailed study of the burn patterns generally helps in CRIME SCENE MANAGEMENT | 50

of damage of a fire, which may contain clues for arson investigations.

3) PHYSICAL EVIDENCE

Introduction

Evidence in general is anything that tends to prove or disprove a point under
investigation or consideration. Physical Evidence is evidence having a physical
or material quality — a tangible article, no matter whether it is microscopic or
macroscopic. It encompasses any and all objects, living or inanimate, solid,
liquid or gas. Physical evidence can alternatively be called as material objects,
material evidence, objective evidence, circumstantial evidence or indirect
evidence in different contexts. Physical Evidence includes all naturally occurring
substances as well as machine/man-made objects in the Universe. It is an established fact that the criminals while committing crimes may leave
traces at the scenes due to carelessness, due to anxiety or due to contact with
other objects and this becomes the basis for scientifically exploiting their cul-
pability. This physical evidence when located, identified, collected, preserved
and forwarded for scientific evaluation and Forensic analysis report is bound
to have enormous potential in linking the criminal, to the crime scene, victim
or any other circumstances leading to the crime. Moreover the evidence col-
lected is based on scientific evolution and has an unbiased report which is uni-
versally acceptable and also stands the test of time.

Purpose of Physical Evidence in Crime Investigation Purpose of Physical Evidence in Crime Investigation

The purpose of physical evidence examination is to provide useful information
for criminal investigators in solving crimes and for courts of law during the ad-
judication of these cases. The following leads can be obtained from the exam-
ination of physical evidence in the process of investigation.

1. Information on the corpus delicti: The corpus delicti (literally means
the “body of the crime”) refers to those essential facts which show that a
crime has taken place.

2. Information on the modus operandi (MO): Many criminals have a
particular modus operandi or (MO), which is their characteristic and signatory
way of committing a crime. Physical evidence can help in establishing an MO. FORENSIC SCIENCE | 54

Linking a suspect with a victim: This linkage is one of the most
important and common physical evidence. Locard’s principle can be
observed as when there is transfer of material from one surface to the other.
In the same way Blood, hairs, clothing fibers, cosmetics and other items
from the victims may be transferred to the perpetrator. Also the Items
found in the suspect’s possession can sometimes be linked to a victim, for
example comparison of bullets from the crime scene with the weapon
seized from the suspect; can be linked. It is also possible that evidence is
transferred from a perpetrator to a victim like in rape, murder and assault
cases. Linking a person to a crime scene: This is also one of the most vital
link in a crime scene investigation. Many types of evidence may be
deposited by the person committing a crime including fingerprints, foot
prints, blood, hair, fibers, soil etc, in the scene of crime. In addition, the
type of weapons or objects used may also leave some evidence, for example;
bullets, empty cartridge cases, tool marks etc. Sometimes evidences from
the crime scene may be carried away intentionally as a stolen property or
unintentionally as a transfer of trace evidence such as carpet fibers, hairs
on the criminal’s shoes or clothing etc,. These materials are extremely useful
in linking an individual to a particular crime scene. Disproving or supporting a witness’ testimony: Physical evidence
analysis can often indicate conclusively, whether a person’s version of a set
of events is credible, or an alibi put forth is convincing or not.
Identification of a suspect: The best evidence for identifying a suspect
is his or her palm prints and evidences such as blood, semen, saliva, skin
tissues etc. collected for DNA Finger Printing examination. A fingerprint
found at a scene, and later identified as belonging to a particular person,
results in an unequivocal identification of that person as having been at the
scene. The term identification when applied to people really means
“individualization” (identifying a single unique source).

Providing investigative leads: Physical evidence analysis can assist the
investigator in pursuing a productive path, by providing clues from the
characteristics of the physical evidence. Identification of a substance: The results of examining a piece of physical
evidence can provide information on the identity of a particular substance.
Crime Scene Reconstruction: Transient evidences such as odour,
temperature (of car engine, fire debris etc), imprints, marks/stains; pattern
evidence like blood stain patterns, skid mark patterns, gunshot residue

FORENSIC SCIENCE | 55

patterns, projectile trajectory patterns, glass fracture patterns etc., and
conditional evidence such as light, smoke, fire, water, piled up news papers
etc., will play a key role in reconstruction of the events surrounding the
crime scene and the nature of offence.

Physical Evidence: Sources and Types

Sources The primary source of physical evidence is generally the crime scene but it is
by no means the only source. Many crimes occur over multiple scenes and many
criminals carry physical evidence away from the actual scene in addition to leav-
ing evidence behind. The victim, suspect and sometimes the witnesses are major
sources of physical evidence. The methodology of search depends on the case
and the scene. The purpose of the search is to look for clues and for evidence
to understand as to what happened during the crime. It is not a random groping
in dark but rather a directed search for objects and materials. Recognizing,
recording, collecting, marking, packaging and preserving the evidence discov-
ered while searching a crime scene is the basic essential duty of the investigating
officer. In cases where the evidence is of a common nature i.e., evidence which
is not hidden is easily collectable, without expert’s assistance, the Investigating is not hidden is easily collectable, without expert’s assistance, the Investigating
Officer should invariably collect all possible forms of physical evidence. For
other kinds of evidence the investigator should take the assistance of FSL ex-
perts or CLUES Team or medico-legal experts. Some types of physical evi-
dence, which are hidden, latent or fragile require scientific procedures for
location, identification, as well as collection and packing. Types

e Impressions: Fingerprints, Palm prints, Foot and Foot wear impressions,
Fabric impressions, Bite marks, Tire marks and Tool marks, Newly damaged
areas, Dents and Breaks, Bullet holes.

e Forensic Serology/Biology: Blood, semen, body fluids, Feces, Vomit,
Spittle, Urine, Cigarette butts, hair, nail scrapings, blood stain patterns etc.

e Trace Evidence: gunshot residues, arson accelerant, paint, glass and fibers.

e Firearms: weapons, gun powder patterns, casings, projectiles, fragments,
pellets, wadding and cartridges.

¢ Question Documents: Notes, letters or papers.

e Computer and other electronic/digital Evidence. FORENSIC SCIENCE | 56

Physical Evidence: Merits and Demerits
Merits

Physical evidence is invariably present in all crimes when properly identified,
lifted and evaluated scientifically. It will have great potential in linking to the
crime / victim /criminal. It is also:

e Time-tested

e Demonstrable

e Universally accepted
e Without bias

e Repeatable

Merits

e Gives clear picture of scene

e Can identify the criminal

e Can describe the sequence of events
e It conclusively establishes the facts

e Itcarries more legal value in the court

De-merits e Can describe the sequence of events
e It conclusively establishes the facts

e Itcarries more legal value in the court

De-merits

e Perceptional variations- Errors in observations, limitation of vision,
power of hearing and perception.

e Memory variations - Vague, Superficial, short-lived, retention, recall, time lapse etc.

FORENSIC SCIENCE | 57

e Expression variations- Exaggeration, Omission, Inability to describe,

confusion etc.

e Class variations - Social, Economic, Political, Religious, Age, Sex, Caste,

status etc.

e Subjective variations - Reluctance, indifference, lack of interest, mental and
intellectual abilities, innocent rustic, pathological liar, fear, friendship, and
other relationships all come under the category of subjective variations.

On the other hand physical evidence which is invariably present in all crimes
when it is properly located, lifted, and evaluated scientifically will have great
potential in linking the crime with the criminal. Moreover this evidence is free
from all pitfalls ad human fragilities.

Guidelines for Handling, Lifting, Packing and Forwarding of Physical Evidence

Handling and Lifting of Physical Evidence

Evidence collected at the scene of crime can be so varied in type, size, delicacy,
and degree of perishability that there can be no set procedure laid down to
cover all aspects.

Few precautions are listed below:

1.

2.
3.

8.

9.

10.

11.
12.

Avoid temptation of picking up an article immediately on arrival at the
crime scene.

Prevent careless destruction of any evidence.

Maintain integrity of the evidence material, i.e., original character of the
evidence.

Ensure chain of possession of evidence collected.
Use a pair of rubber/ disposable gloves while handing every article.
Handle articles as little as possible so that no clue is lost or damaged.

In case of a flat object like a piece of wood or glass sheet only the edges
should be handled. In case of a flat object like a piece of wood or glass sheet only the edges
should be handled.

In the case of tumbler, bottle or phial only the rim and the bottom should
be touched, using a finger tips at top and bottom of article.

In the case of dagger or knife, the tips of the handle and the blade should
be handled by the finger tips.

Fire arms can be lifted with the tips of the fingers held against the ends of
the barrel and the butt.

A fired projectile should be lifted only with a rubber tipped forceps.
Paper, hairs, fibers, small pieces of glass or any small sized solid object
should be lifted with rubber tipped forceps. FORENSIC SCIENCE | 62

13. Dust or other powdered substances should be collected with a brush or a
vacuum cleaner.

14. Explosives and bombs should not be touched or handled unless one is sure
that there will be no explosion by simple handling.

15. Blood, semen, liquids, chemicals, vomit and excreta etc. should be collected
with the help of a spatula (like spoon).

Packing of Physical Evidence

The following points should be borne in mind with regard to the packing

of exhibits:

1. The exhibits packed will not be damaged by knocking against each other if
properly padded with cotton, thermo coal ete.

2. Each article should be packed separately and labeled so that the individually
and identity are maintained.

3. The container should be strong enough to avoid damage in transit.

— F

4. The packing should be secure enough to avoid contamination of the
sample. — F

4. The packing should be secure enough to avoid contamination of the
sample.

5. As far as possible all exhibits/samples belonging to one case should be
packed separately and enclosed in a box, or a parcel.

6. Parcels with samples belonging to different cases should not be enclosed
in a single parcel.

7. The exhibits are packed in such a manner that they cannot be damaged
when unpacked.

8. Each finger print card to be packed separately in a plastic L folder.

9. The exhibits are contaminated by packing in unclean containers.

10. Use suitable lids, cork or stoppers to avoid spillage and leakage.

Labeling of Physical Evidence

1. Do not paste labels on weapons, tools,
clothing’s etc,.

indicated and marked as ‘Handle with Care’
to avoid damage of the case property.

3. The exhibits are sent through a special
messenger as far as possible.

4. If the parcel is sent by post it should be
properly packed, labeled, addressed, and
preferably insured.

5. Postal regulations should be kept in mind
while dispatching exhibits like firearms and
explosives.

Sealing

1. All parcels should be carefully sealed by the
dispatching officers before packing in such
a manner that they cannot be opened
without destroying the seals.

2, All knots on the parcel should be properly
sealed.

3. The impression of the seal should be
legible.

4. The seal should be kept in safe custody of

of keys, coins, buttons, etc. 5. The seal could be a private or official seal but should never be an impression
of keys, coins, buttons, etc.

6. It is mandatory that the facsimile of the seal always be sent along with the

Transportation/ Forwarding

1.

All preserved, packed, labeled and sealed Physical evidence should be
transported carefully to Forensic Science Laboratory for forensic analysis
through a messenger or any police personnel. FORENSIC SCIENCE | 64

2. The parcel indicated and marked as ‘Handle with Care’ has to be
transported with maximum care to avoid damage of the case property.

3. Damp/Heavy items are packed and transported accordingly.

4. Items are packed and placed in such a position that the chance of damage,
alteration, contamination or loss is minimized.

5. Items that are damaged negligently during the transportation will not stand
firm with evidential value and will not serve as accurate representation of
what was actually collected from the scene of crime.

Chain of Custody of Physical Evidence

The term ‘chain of custody’
means maintaining a record
of every article of evidence,
from the point of discovery at
a crime scene to its collection
and transport to the forensic
laboratory for examination,
and its temporary storage till
it is finally submitted to the
Court along with the report
of analysis. The ‘chain of custody’ must be established whenever evidence is presented
in Court as an exhibit. Otherwise, the evidence may be inadmissible in the
Court of Law. For this reason there must be a systematic record of the names
of the persons in possession of the exhibits like through whom the exhibits
were enrooted to whom they were delivered, with their initials, time and date
of receipt and delivery.

Each person in the chain of custody is responsible for the care, safekeeping
and preservation of evidence while it is under his control. The chain is
maintained when the evidence is received from another officer.

Introduction

Forensic Science in its broadest sense is the application of techniques and
principles of science for the purpose of administration of criminal justice
system. Chemistry is an integral part of forensic science. Forensic scientists
must understand the principles, concepts and techniques of chemistry to
perform their duties in an effective manner. Scientists of all types are often
interested in the composition of a substance. However, the type of substance
may vary according to their nature of work. Like, pharmaceutical scientists
identify the substances in a drug sample to set their expiry dates. Similarly
forensic scientists identify substance to connect suspects to crimes or to
reconstruct a crime i.e. to determine how a crime has been committed.

History History

The very first history of Forensic Chemistry began with analysis of poisons.
In early times, there are evidences of the use of poisons by early Egyptians,
Greeks and Romans. At that time, poisons were used both for murder and for
the execution of death penalty; the philosopher Socrates was sentenced to death
by drinking hemlock. Before the development of any systematic scientific
procedure of criminal investigation, circumstantial and hearsay evidences were
usually employed to ascertain the guilt of any person.

Arsenic was a commonly used poison during Roman times. The first instance
of an actual chemical test for poison is the Blandy trial conducted during 1752.

Chemistry

Chemistry is “the branch of science that deals with the study of the
composition of matter and different changes that it undergoes”. Chemistry

Chemistry is “the branch of science that deals with the study of the
composition of matter and different changes that it undergoes”.

Forensic chemistry is “a specialized sub-field of forensic science involving
the application of techniques and principles of chemistry to the field of foren-
sic investigation”. It involves complex procedures of chemical analysis that are FORENSIC CHEMISTRY | 70

used to identify elements and compounds. Identification procedures are highly
reliable and are frequently based on the physical and chemical properties of
the substance supported by the data obtained from analytical analysis.

Forensic chemistry

Forensic chemistry is the application of principles of chemistry to law
enforcement and criminal justice system. There are various analytical methods
which may be used to find out the chemical changes that occurred during any
incident, and in this way it helps in the reconstruction of crime scene. Forensic
chemistry meets the need of both the scientific and the legal communities by
innovations in its research, practice, and presentation. Every facet of the criminal justice system would suffer without forensic chem-
istry. Forensic chemistry acts as armed force in the pursuit of justice. Forensic
chemistry gives voice to the silent witness of physical evidence. It plays a critical
role in the administration of justice, not only aids in the investigation and pros-
ecution of crimes but also assist in meeting up the ends of justice.

Role of a Forensic Chemist

* The ability of forensic professionals to establish the presence of a link
between accused and crime by matching physical evidence collected from
the scene of crime and those which are obtained from the victim or accused
(Law of Comparison), strengthen the case of prosecution. * Forensic chemists perform different analyses to identify materials in order
to determine the nature and composition of such evidence. A highly trained
forensic chemist is capable of determining the composition and nature of
materials and predicting the source as well as matching evidences collected

FORENSIC CHEMISTRY | 71

from the crime scene with the control sample.

Forensic chemists have a crucial role in forensic science laboratories and
when required they are often called on in the courts, to provide testimony
as expert witnesses. In these cases, the chemist may compare questioned
evidence to control samples and may be asked to give an expert opinion in
court on the basis of grounds on which such opinion is based. Their
academic credentials, along with years of experience to deal with such
analysis, render their opinion valuable and believable. The fact that most samples that are submitted for analysis are contaminated
with dirt or debris and several other contaminants, this poses a major
challenge before forensic chemist. Every substance that is collected from
the scene of crime has a unique composition that can ultimately be
identified. Arsonists, for example, often use accelerants such as kerosene
or gasoline to accelerate the rate of combustion and each accelerant has its
own unique composition.

A forensic chemist may search for and collect burnt and unburnt material
samples, extract the volatile hydrocarbons from them, and may use gas
chromatography to separate the components of these samples.

Scope and Significance in Forensic Chemistry

The principles of chemistry play quite a crucial role in each division of
Forensic Science Laboratory. A few of them are as follows-

Examination of metal alloys and metal fragments for identification and
comparison. Examination of metal alloys and metal fragments for identification and
comparison.

Examination of petroleum products like diesel, petrol, and kerosene.

Analysis of dyes, paints, inks, fillers, binders and various other chemicals
to their identity, composition, quality as well as quantity.

Examination of low standard construction material like cement, bricks, etc.
as well as the adulteration in these building materials.

Analysis of various narcotic, designer and abused drugs like bhang, opium,
ganja, LSD etc. as well as illicit liquors.

Determination of alcohol in blood and urine in cases of suspected
drunkenness and analysis of fermented wash, varnish, etc. in prohibition
and excise cases.

Analysis of pesticides for their identification.

Examination of inflammable material in suspected cases of arson, dowry
deaths, etc.

Analysis of explosives, firearms and ammunition. FORENSIC CHEMISTRY | 72

Forensic Examination of Petroleum Products for Adulteration

Common Adulterants

In India, Adulteration of fuel is very booming, due to different price of product
with similar concentrations. The dealers of fuel do this adulteration in order to
make extreme profit from product ignoring the damages to vehicles and harmful
effects to life of humans. For example, when adulteration of kerosene takes
place with petrol it can be very hazardous as it can be highly inflammable. The
main effect of fuel adulteration is on vehicle that uses such adulterated fuel. This
causes increase cases of tailpipe emission and hence cause engine knock.

The frequent blending of lubricant into kerosene, kerosene into petrol/ diesel
and lubricant into diesel are very common type of petroleum adulteration.

Key Statutory Provisions

Petroleum and petroleum products are covered under Section 2 (a) (viii) of
Essential Commodities Act of 1955, amended from time to time. All orders
and notifications are issued by the Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas in
exercise of powers conferred by Section 3 of the said Act.

Adulteration is the illegal addition of any foreign substance into motor Gaso-
line / high speed diesel as per “The Motor Spirit and High Speed Diesel” (Pre-
vention of Malpractices in Supply and Distribution) Order 1993 by Section 3
of Essential Commodities Act (E.C. Act).

The person involve in malpractices of adulteration can be penalized under
the Essential Commodities Act or under section 420 IPC and also under Pe-
troleum Act 1934, unlawful possession, breach of contract, pilferage, etc.

All Central/States/UTs Forensic Science Laboratories are authorized under
Schedule HI of Clause 8(5) of EC Act 1955 for testing of Petroleum Product
Samples. Forensic Examination (Preliminary) of Petroleum Products for adulteration are based on
the following type of characteristics:

° Density

* Flash Point

* Boiling Point

* Ultra Violet Lamp

* — Cetane Number

* Octane Number

scientific tool and techniques-

* Gas Chromatography

Introduction

Toxicology deals with the science that embodies the knowledge of the
sources, characters and properties of poisons, the symptoms they produce,
and the nature of their fatal effects, the remedial measures that should be
taken to combat their actions or effects. The word ‘Toxicology’ is derived
from the Greek word-‘toxicon’, which was used as a poisonous substance
to arrowheads. The forensic toxicologist is expected to detect and identify
poisons. A poison is defined as the substance, which is capable of producing
injury or death when absorbed. Appropriate dosages can differentiate poison
and also the remedial measures. The term ‘forensic toxicology’ covers any
application of the science and study of poisons to the elucidation of
questions that occur in judicial proceedings. Any chemical can produce injury or death under certain conditions.
Hence, a poison can be defined as a substance that is capable of producing
detrimental effects on a living organism. As a result, there may be a change
in the structure of the substance or functional processes, which may produce
injury or even death. The toxicologist is specially a trained expert to examine
the role of such substances and their adverse effects.

The variety of potential adverse effects and the diversity of chemicals
present in our environment contribute to make toxicology a very broad field
of science. Therefore, toxicologists are usually specialized to handle various
areas of toxicology. The professional activities of toxicologists fall into four
main categories; Forensic Toxicology, Industrial Toxicology, Clinical
Toxicology and Environmental Toxicology. Forensic toxicology emerged on the hybrid of analytical Chemistry and
toxic principle effects. Forensic toxicologists are also primarily concerned
with the medico legal aspects of the harmful effects of chemicals on human
and animals.

‘Taruvu Suresh 6 NARCOTICS

Introduction

A drug can be defined as ‘a natural or synthetic substance that is used to
produce physiological effects in man’. But in modern context drugs mean
something different to each person. To some drugs are needed to sustain and
prolong life, in some cases drugs provide an escape from pressures of life. The
law enforcement agency is concerned with the later type of drug which is widely
abused.

The word ‘narcotic’ is derived from a Greek word “Narcotikos” means
ethargy or sluggishness. According to the “Pharmacologists” narcotics are the
drugs, which adversely affect the activity of the central nervous system. In
Medicine they are used for the relief of the pain, to induce sleep and to treat
psychiatric disorders under medical supervision, but the misuse or over dose
leads to dependence, addiction, toxic symptoms and death. Examples are
opium and their derivatives like morphine, heroin and codeine ete. Dangerous drugs or psychotropic substances are non-narcotic drugs. These
affect the mental state of the abuser, by producing depressant or stimulant
effect on the central nervous system or hallucinogenic effect. LSD,
amphetamines and barbiturates belong to this category of drugs.

Classification of Drugs

To meet the growing menace of drug abuse, the Government of India
promulgated the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances (NDPS) Act in
1985. This act has been further amended in 1988 to provide detention powers
to the Central and State Government enforcement agencies. Narcotics and
Dangerous Drugs can be classified into three parts

1. Depressants
2. Stimulants and

3. Hallucinogens

NARCOTICS | 98

Depressants

Depressants Act upon the central nervous system and slow down its activity.
Drug abusers term it as “high” which means a state of well-being and comfort
which is an escape from the reality with which they cannot cope. This imagined
state of mind is called euphoria. The following drugs fall in this category:

a. Opium: Opium is the milky-
white juice that is obtained
from the pod of the poppy
plant. The substance darkens
to a blackish brown colour and
thickness upon exposure to air.
In India, it is grown in some
parts of Rajasthan, Madhya
Pradesh and Uttar Pradesh.
Raw opium has a distinctive
and pungent odour.

It is most commonly abused by smoking in long stemmed pipes. Chronic
use of opium leads to both physical and mental dependence and tolerance
builds up. Withdrawal symptoms, like becoming nervous, restless, anxious,
sweating, running eyes and nose develop when the drug supply is cut off. b. Morphine: Morphine is obtained from raw opium base through a chemical
process in which ten kilogram of raw opium yields one kilogram of
morphine base. It usually appears as an odourless white crystalline powder
on the illicit market. It may also appear in tablet, capsule and liquid form.

Morphine is usually administered by injection with hypodermic syringe.
When consumed morphine creates both mental and physical dependence in NARCOTICS | 99

the user. Euphoric state is obtained with morphine, and the user may present

a sleepy or relaxed appearance with constricted pupils of the ey

white powder which does not have any known medical use. Heroin in

and capsules.

It is 10 to 15 times more potent than morphine. Heroin is either injected
or sniffed into the body. The effects of heroin are similar to those of opium
and morphine, but due to its high potency its effect on the user is more

Stimulants

Stimulants excite or stimulate the central nervous system. They can be obtained
from natural sources and can be synthesized. Some of the examples are as
follows:

a. Cocaine: Cocaine is the active alkaloid obtained from the coca leaves.
When extracted and refined, cocaine is a white crystalline powder. It
causes mental but no physical dependence. Tolerance does not develop. Cocaine produces a sense of euphoria and a feeling of increased alertness
and vigor accompanied by the suppression of hunger, fatigue, and boredom.
Cocaine is usually sniffed, but it also injected. The after effects of cocaine in-
clude anxiety, restlessness, and feelings of depression. NARCOTICS | 101

b. Amphetamine: Amphetamines are synthetic non-narcotic dangerous drugs
that stimulate the central nervous system. They are widely used by truck
drivers, night watchmen and students to keep them awake or increase
alertness, which is often followed by a decrease in fatigue and a loss of
appetite.

However once the effects of drug are over depression may set in.
Amphetamines are available in capsule, tablet or liquid form. They are taken
either orally or by injection.

c. Methamphetamine: Methamphetamine is another form of amphetamine
called “speed” because of its rapid stimulation tof the central nervous
system. Methamphetamine appears in capsule, tablet, or liquid form.
