Computer Crime Prevention Measures

1. Establishing a secure computer center. Computer Crime Prevention Measures

1. Establishing a secure computer center.

2. Limited area to computer programs, operations and data.

3. Establish procedures for back up of data files and programs.
4. Change access codes and user ID at frequent intervals.

5. Maintain log of computer users.

6. Provide adequate fire prevention measures.

7. Keep reliable and trustworthy staff to manage the center and rotate their
job tasks periodically.

8. Install fire wall in the system to prevent standing of data.

Search and seizure of Computers

Preparation for the Seizure

As with any search and seizure operation good preparation is the key to success.
Obtain as much information as possible about the type of computer system,
the location of the equipment within the building, the layout of the building, COMPUTER FORENSICS | 350

the number of people present etc. If possible find out the number of
computers that may need to be examined and the size of the hard disks.

To seize or not to seize
‘There are basically two ways of dealing with computers when on a search operation

* Seize the computer and its associated hardware and forward it to laboratory
where the contents can be examined which is a recommended procedure.

¢ If the IO has the knowledge of computer, then take the imaging of the
open windows on site without taking possession of the computer itself.

f the system belongs to an accused the ideal situation is to take possession of
the computer and forward it to Forensic laboratory. However, it may not be
possible to do this when the computer does not belong to the accused, or it
might be vital to the owners business for it to remain on site.

How to handle a ‘live’ computer How to handle a ‘live’ computer

n order to seize a computer it must first be switched off. The general rules is
that if a computer is turned on at the scene then switch it off by removing
power cable from CPU and then switch off the main switch. Do not allow the
owner to touch it since you do not know if they have set certain keystrokes to
perform nonstandard operations of a destructive nature which may handle
the Investigation process.

What to seize and where to look

First identify the computer and its peripherals like cable modems, hardware-
keyboard, monitor, printer scanners etc. The general rule is to take possession
of all the attached hardware. Sometimes it so happen that an item is needed at a later stage for some
previously unidentified reason. For example, it may need to have the printer
examined to establish if it produced a particular document. Items such as
modems and network adapters will be needed if the system proves to be non-
standard and must be rebuilt at a later date in the laboratory. While keyboards
and monitors are fairly standard, there are some differences so as a precaution,
it is cautioned to collect them before hand. Also if possible look carefully for
floppy disks and portable storage media devices.

They may be placed anywhere in the site and many a times have proven to
be vital in a number of cases. Over smart suspects will often store information

COMPUTER FORENSICS | 351

on floppy disks that they will not keep on the hard disk. If you find floppy
disks always pack them according to their location at the scene. For example,
put the disk that was near the monitor in a separate bag to the ones found in
the desk drawer. Clearly mark the location of the disks on each packet.

Floppy disks, zip disks, optical cartridges and sometimes even CD's may also
contain backup files which can be restored at a later date in the laboratory.
These can be most useful for establishing significant event time sequences.

Talk to the owner or operator of the system Talk to the owner or operator of the system

While all this is going on, it is a good to talk to the owner or operator of the
system and ask if there are any password protection devices, access control
systems that need to know about. If there are any, make a note of them together
with the name of the person you discussed with. In practice it is rare to find
desktop computers protected with passwords but quite common on portable
and notebook computers. The presence of a password can itself indicate that
there is something suspicious and 'worth hiding’ on a machine.

Do not attempt to examine the contents of a computer directly at the end
under any circumstances!

Photograph and recording equipment layout

In case of seizing more than one computer system, make sure which peripherals
belong to which processing box. This can be done by labeling each cable as it
is disconnected and removed. Use digital camera to photograph and record the
layout before and after taking out the cables Bagging, tagging and removing equipment

Once disconnected, the system should be placed in high density polythene bags
faraday bags, bubble bag or anti magnetic and anti static current baggages. Each
parcel should be sealed and labeled with details such as description of contents
and location. Disk drives are fairly robust but transport and handling the system
box should be very gentle. All equipment should be handled carefully.

Storage of seized equipment

A record should be maintained as chain of custody of all actions and movements
involving the item together with details of any seals, to prove the integrity of the
IO. Seized equipment should be stored in a clean, dry and secure location. Stored COMPUTER FORENSICS | 352

equipment should be kept away from excessive heat or cold (i.e. radiators or ait
conditioning vents), any magnetic fields (i.e. functioning of electrical equipment
ot loudspeakers) and it should not be allowed to get dusty or dirty.

Dealing with UNIX/Linux Operating systems

When dealing with UNIX/ Linux systems it is not advisable to unplug the
power cable directly when the computer is switched ON it is better to follow
the regular procedure of shutting down by using SHUTDOWN command, as
the files which are currently running and not saved cannot be retrieved if the
system is shutdown by unplugging the power cable. Deleted files cannot be
retrieved in UNIX as in Windows operating system.

Dealing with large networks Dealing with large networks

n case of seizing a large network, it is essential to obtain specialist help from
someone familiar with a similar system. The way in which the system is configured
may be crucial to successful examination at a later date. It may also not be
necessary to seize all parts of the system and technical assistance may identify
only those parts that are required. It is preferred that person from cyber crime
police station or scientist from laboratory should be requested for the help.

Prevention of common mistakes by 10's
Operating the Computer System in Question t is a general tendency exhibited by the 10's to try operating the computer
system in question. This activity can sometimes prove fatal as it is relatively
easy for criminals to rig their computers to destroy hard disk drive content or
specific files by planting decoy or modifying the operating system. For example
the simple DIR command to view the directory contents can be easily modified
to reformat the hard disk. After the destruction there will be nothing left to
prove that there was a modified program in the hard disk because neither the
malicious program nor the contents of the hard disk are available for analysis.
Therefore it is absolutely essential for an 1O to keep away from operating the
Computer system in question using the native OS. It is not unusual for an IO
to take the help of a nearby computer operator, someone like local DTP
operator, nearby Cyber cafe Owner or any other computer literate who is to take the help of a nearby computer operator, someone like local DTP
operator, nearby Cyber cafe Owner or any other computer literate who is
unaware of the forensic procedures of seizure of a computer system. But such
practice can prove very costly many a times. Therefore the golden rule would
be not to operate the questioned computer system using its native OS.

COMPUTER FORENSICS | 353

Getting help from the Computer Owner

It is potentially a serious mistake to allow the owner of the computer to
operate the computer in question.

Improper Transportation of the Computer Evidence

Another mistake generally committed by investigation agencies is not taking
proper precautions while transporting the computer evidence. Computer
evidence is highly sensitive to mechanical shocks electro-static interferences.
Therefore it is essential for the IO to take proper precautions and prevent

damage of electronic evidence while transporting.

Hacking

Hacking is a broader term and can be defined as gaining entry into a computer
system without the permission with an intention to cause loss, steal, or destroy
the data contained in it. It is often done by people who are well versed with
computer technologies by exploiting some of the vulnerabilities that are present
in the computer system. This involves various methods of acquiring sensitive

using them to access the computer system.

Hackers use various applications or programs that can penetrate the
defence mechanisms employed by the target computer system and send back
the critical information like computer configuration, user names, IP
addresses, MAC addresses, etc., which can be used by the hacker to gain
entry into the system itself. These applications may be in the form of
Trojans, malware, worms, and viruses, which will install in the targeted
system and compromise its security. After hacking and gaining entry into
the computer system, the hacker can gain administrative rights and can do
anything with the data contained in it. The computer systems can also be
used to infect and destroy other systems.

Re) / QUESTIONED DOCMENTS

Introduction In the world of business and literacy most of the transactions take place
through documents. Consequently, documents are assuming an increasingly
important role in our society. The discipline of forensic document
examination, often referred to as “questioned documents” is frequently
associated with white-collar crimes. Questioned document examination
pertaining to documents that are potentially disputed in a court of law. The
examination’s primary purpose is to provide evidence about suspicious or
questioned documents using a variety of scientific principles and methods.
Document examination might include alterations, obliterations, paper
analysis, forgery, origin, determining authenticity, or any other questions.
However, in practice, this area of forensic science can be used in a wide array
of cases from medical malpractice to forgeries to homicides. Roughly 70%
of the physical evidence relates to disputed documents. Many forms of of cases from medical malpractice to forgeries to homicides. Roughly 70%
of the physical evidence relates to disputed documents. Many forms of
writing may be encountered as evidence in the course of investigation. Armed with sophisticated technology, forensic document examiners can
look into the visible and invisible marks on a document to extract an
abundance of details that may verify authenticity or provide additional
information which may be crucial to the investigation. The digital age has
made the work of forensic document examiners even more important. With
the availability of powerful software programs such as Adobe Photoshop,
Acrobat and others, it has become significantly easier for criminals to create
and manipulate all manner of fraudulent documents from contracts to
currency.

Document

Document can be understood as any material containing marks, symbols, or
signs either visible, partially visible that may present or ultimately convey a
meaning to someone. This may be in the form of pencil writing, ink writing,
typewriting, or printing on paper.

QUESTIONED DOCUMENTS | 364

Questioned Document

Definition: Any document or object that contains a handwritten, typewritten
or printed marking, whose source or authenticity is in doubt or which is under
scrutiny is referred to as a “questioned document”. Such a broad definition
covers all the written and printed materials that are normally encountered in
our daily, social and business activities.

Document section receives the following document exhibits for
document examinations:

1. Handwritings, signatures, initials etc.

2. Erasures (Mechanical and Chemical).

3. Secret and invisible writings.

4. Indented writings or pressure marks.

5. Alterations of writings.

6. Additions and deletions of writings.

7. Traced, simulated and other forms of forgery.

Forged rubber stamp or metal impressions, postal cancellation marks.

9. Anonymous letters. 7. Traced, simulated and other forms of forgery.

Forged rubber stamp or metal impressions, postal cancellation marks.

9. Anonymous letters.

10. False Certificates, marks lists, ration cards, passports and pass books,
vehicle registration books, wills and agreements, ‘Traveler cheques and
credit cards, etc.

11. Forged or tampered bank cheques and drafts.

12. White-collar crimes such as land scams, share certificates etc.

13. Torn, mutilated and charred documents.

14. Examination of ink and paper.

15. Examination of typed or printed matter etc.

Nature of Problems

Questioned document problems are varied in nature. The important
ones relate to:

1.
2.

Identification of handwriting, signatures and detection of forgery.

Examination of writing materials like pen, paper, ink etc. QUESTIONED DOCUMENTS | 365

3. Evaluation of erasers, alterations, additions and obliterations.
Decipherment of indented and charred documents.
To determine order and age of writing.

6. Evaluation of handwriting and typescript.

Identification of Handwriting

Identification of handwriting is the most important branch of questioned
documents. It may be necessary to identify the signature of the text in a
document to ascertain, whether it is by the hand of a suspect or the victim,
whether the signatures are traced or disguised.

Basic Principles of Identification of Handwriting

The basic philosophy behind Handwriting Identification is comparison of class
and Individual characteristics of the given handwriting sample. ‘The first step
in any legal handwriting examination is to observe the general appearance of
the questioned writing as compared with the genuine standard writing. By this
method the conspicuous characteristics are determined.

Major principles of handwriting Major principles of handwriting

There are some major principles of handwriting which the practitioners and
Forensic Document expert have established.

1. No two persons write exactly alike because no two persons have the same
experience in life and therefore their writings will always vary. Normally it
is said that writing is influenced with experience, environment, mental
condition, physical condition and emotional make up.

No one person writes exactly the same way twice.

3. A writer is to able to imitate all the features of another person’s handwriting
while simultaneously writing at the same relative speed and skill level as the
writer he is seeking to imitate.

4. In simulating another’s writing, the simulator will try to imitate those
features that are most striking to his eye. He frequently either disregards
those features that are less conspicuous to him or fails to imitate them
successfully 5. For those writing where the writer successfully disguises his normal
handwriting habits or where he imitates or traces, the writing habits of
another writer on learning, it is virtually impossible to identify the imitator.

QUESTIONED DOCUMENTS | 366

Characteristics of Handwriting

eneral Characteristics

Handwriting is an acquired art like painting, dancing or cycling. The early stages

hat accompany the learning and practicing of writing are characterized by a
‘onscious effort on the part of a student to copy standard letter forms. He takes

mental impression of the model before him and makes an effort to translate

it through the muscles of his hand, as writing. After practice, proficiency is
cquired and the writing gets set and mature. It is then no longer effortful, but

lmost a reflex action. A perfectly correct picture of the model is not grasped

by the mind nor reproduced though the muscles. There are deviations in both
these aspects. These mental and muscular deviations result in a nerve-muscle
reaction pattern and give the writing its individuality. The handwriting that is

haracteristic of an individual is the product of a number of influencing factors, haracteristic of an individual is the product of a number of influencing factors,

each of which contributes to the final form, style, and characteristics of the

Individual Characteristics

t is a well-established fact that the unconscious handwriting of two
different individuals can never be duplicated. Individual Characteristics

t is a well-established fact that the unconscious handwriting of two
different individuals can never be duplicated.

ndividual variations associated with physical and mental state of the writer
make it extremely unlikely that any two individuals can exactly reproduce
the writing characteristics. Thus, variations are expected in angularity, slope,
speed, pressure, letter and word spacing, relative dimensions of letters,
connections, pen movement, writing skill, line quality etc. Besides, personal
habits like margins, spacing, crowding, insertions, alignment, spelling,
punctuation, and grammar can together help individualize the writer.

referred above of both the known and questioned documents are examined
and compared.

A single handwriting characteristic feature cannot be taken as basis for a
positive comparison. A single handwriting characteristic feature cannot be taken as basis for a
positive comparison.

The final conclusion of identity or non-identity has to be based on a
sufficient number of common characteristics between the known and
questioned writing. QUESTIONED DOCUMENTS | 367

Sometimes, it may be difficult for the expert to arrive at a positive conclusion,
when a known writing made available for comparison has insufficient number
of characteristics. Difficulties may also arise when the expert is presented with
questioned writings containing only a few words, or text deliberately written in
a crude unnatural form, or very carefully written to disguise the writer’s natural
style. Although it may be relatively easy to change one’s writing habits for a few
words or sentences, the task of disguising becomes more and more difficult
with lengthy text or writing.

Factors Affecting Handwriting

Handwriting being an acquired art is affected by a number of internal
and external factors. Some of these factors are:

* Writing materials: Paper, pen, ink, pencil, writing support, seat etc., can
contribute to variations in handwriting. * Writing materials: Paper, pen, ink, pencil, writing support, seat etc., can
contribute to variations in handwriting.

* Physical or psychological disturbances: Excitement, illness, fear, pain,
injury to the hands, and influence of drugs or alcohol can adversely affect
the writing characteristics.

* Age: As age advances some of the old characteristics of writings are
modified or dropped and a few new characteristics are acquired. Old age
may set in deterioration in strokes and poor form of letters.

* Natural variations: No two specimens of writing prepared by one person
are identical in every detail. Variation is an inherent part of natural writing.
If a person writes the same word or signature a number of times, the
reproductions in minutest detail are never alike. These deviations within
certain limits, in form, size, proportion, slant, etc., of characters and in the
combination of characters are called natural variations.

Standards Standards

Hand writings or signatures or initials etc., written by a known person taken
for the purpose of comparison with the questioned are known as standards.
Standards also include typed/printed scripts, seals and stamp impressions ete.,
taken from a known soutce.

There are two types of Standards

¢ Admitted Writings or Non-request standards
* Specimen Writings or Request standards

QUESTIONED DOCUMENTS | 368

Admitted writings

Admitted writings are those written by a person in his/her routine course of
activity/business, which are freely written, natural and undistorted. Such
writings are normally available in case of employees, students, working class
people, professionals, business men etc. The most suitable admitted standards
are those which belong to the same general class (post-card with post-card)
and contemporary (near about date/month/year) to the questioned
documents. The investigating officer should endeavor to collect standards,
which would enable the expert to compare “LIKE WITH LIKE” ice., initials
with initials, signatures with signatures, writings with writings of the same
language, cheques with cheques and so on and so forth depending on the
nature of the documents.

The admitted standard material can be procured in the following forms
of documents The admitted standard material can be procured in the following forms
of documents

* Personal Correspondence: Post cards, inland letters, applications for
employment / loans / ration cards / passports / diaries / account books,
services like electricity, telephone, etc.

* Academic Records: School/College/applications, answer scripts, notes,
records, project reports etc.

¢ Financial and Business Documents: Pass book, bank cheques, applications
for demand drafts, withdrawal forms, account opening forms, specimen
signature cards, ledgers, receipts etc.

* Vocational and Professional Documents: Official correspondence, leave
letters, applications for loans, (P.F., LIC etc.,) attendance register, legal
documents, ete.

* Miscellaneous Documents: Railway/bus reservation forms etc.

Specimen writings * Miscellaneous Documents: Railway/bus reservation forms etc.

Specimen writings

Specimens are the writings specially written by the suspect for the purpose of
comparison with the disputed documents. This is usually done either in the
open court or in the presence of presiding officer. However the specimen
writing of the suspect can be taken only if he volunteers to give. For collection
of such specimens, there are some essential points to be followed to facilitate
expert’s examination meaningful, objective and conclusive, as indicated below;

* The suspect should be seated in a comfortable posture to ensure comfort
and natural writing conditions. QUESTIONED DOCUMENTS | 369

He/she should be supplied with similar paper and writing instruments
(pen/ball pen) as that of the questioned.

The suspect should not be shown the questioned document

The dictation should be given to the suspect at different intervals to prevent
the suspect from imitating the same style.

After each dictation, the sheet of specimens should be removed, and a fresh
sheet of paper to be supplied.

The suspect should be encouraged to write the word combination and
numerals as present in the questioned sample several times.

At least 20 specimen signatures / writings on different sheets should be taken.
In case of deliberate efforts to disguise, more specimens should be taken.
The specimen writings/signature should be taken in the open court and
attested by the Judicial Officer/Magistrate.

Note: While collecting standards 1.O. should take necessary care to prove

their authorship in the court of law.

Marking of documents

The process of segregating the disputed portion of text from out of the total
document by marking distinctively for comparison is known as ‘Marking of
documents’. The questioned portions should be encircled and numbered as
Ql, Q2, Q3,...... respectively with red color pencil , whereas the standard
portions should be encircled and numbered as S1, S2, S . respectively with
blue color pencil.

Basic principles of Signatures

* The principles applicable to general handwriting are also applicable to
signatures. Besides, certain other characteristics not common to other
classes of writing require special consideration.

* A signature is a combined result of cumulative effect of a number of
factors such as muscular control, coordination, health, age, frequency of
writing, temperament etc. All these factors get blended in the signature.

Forensic Handwriting and Signature Identification

Handwriting Identification is a discriminatory process that derives from the
compression of writing habits and an evaluation of significance of their
similarities or differences. Handwriting comparison is the most frequently
requested type of document examination. It occurs in many phases of human
activity throughout life. As children, we are taught a system of handwriting.
Initially we are urged to copy the system as closely as possible. During the later
stage we tend to move away from the standard form and consciously or
subconsciously experiment with new letter forms and unique combinations of
designs. When we become mature individuals we pay less attention to the letter
formation and more attention to the content of the message. Throughout the
process of development of writing personal habits contribute their own
characteristics, and writing becomes as individualistic as speech and mannerisms. Identification of handwriting is dependent on the assumption that certain
deeply set characteristics which a writer develops in him over his lifetime are QUESTIONED DOCUMENTS | 371

consistently and consciously reproduced in a person’s handwriting. Even if he
tries to disguise, the peculiarities come out unknowingly. When a particular
piece of handwriting is called into question the handwriting expert will examine
the writing. A document examiner with the aid of the hand held magnifiers and
with a microscope compares questioned handwriting or signatures with the
known standards.

Every normal individual when looks at writing and signatures first notices
the appearances and slant of writing or signature, but a document expert tries
to think beyond the most obvious characteristics and studies the writing in
depth in a conspicuous way. Handwriting Identification is completely based on
the comparison of class and individual characteristics. It is similar to that of
fingerprint Identification. The gross features of writing are class characteristics. Ordway Hilton defines class characteristics as “Not all characteristics
encountered in a document examination are peculiar to a single person or thing
and one that is common to a group may be described as class characteristics”.

Individual characteristics are those less conspicuous special features in
writing. Any character in writing or any writing or any writing habit may be
modified and individualized by different writers in many different ways and in
many varying degrees and the writing individuality of any particular writer is
made up of all these common and uncommon characteristics and habits.

The comparison of handwriting and signatures is done with the assistance

letter. When the genuine signature does not contain any pen lifts consistently,

The Starting and Ending Strokes in the forgery show Hesitations through the
tremors of lack of firmness. These hesitations in the starting strokes occur
because the forger is not certain whether he will be able to do the job efficiently
or not. In the Ending stoke he fears whether he has been able to forge the
signature well or not. In genuine these strokes are done rather more freely than
rest of the writing.

therefore, pays more attention to the inconspicuous characteristics and line quality. Erasures

Charred Documents

Deciphering documents that have been charred or partially charred is often
possible. For this reason, such material should be collected very carefully and
submitted to the laboratory for examination.

Following techniques are used for their decipherment:

* Visual examination with oblique or side illumination.

¢ If the original ink contained carbon or other infrared opaque ink and the
document is not fully charred, the writing is deciphered through infrared
photography.

* Sometimes silver nitrate solution may help read the charred writing.

* Keeping a charred document pressed on either side by unexposed
photographic plates for nearly two weeks and then developing the normal
way may produce photographic negative of the original matter.

The age of writing can be determined from the age of ink. The change in

The use of a particular pen may point to the age of the document in some
rare cases, ¢.g., the ball pen came into use in India only after 1950 is highly
unlikely.

Distinct marks on revenue stamps change in seals from time to time etc.,
help to approximate the age of the document.

The condition of the material may shed some light on the likely age of the
document, i.e., the changes that may have occurred in some constituent of
the document over a period of time. Taking into account the anachronistic features revealed in the fabricated
document such as, the use of wrong type of paper or writing instrument
or the information available on the document, which ought not to have
been there at the supposed time of writing like postal pin code, change in
the name of state, city or even the road or street etc.
An International Ink Library has been established by the US Secret Services
since 1988. Samples of about 6000 inks have been collected, which can be
used for determining the age of old documents. The methods are both
destructive and non-destructive type using Ultraviolet, Infrared and
Chemical examinations. This facility for dating documents can be availed
by all countries.

Order of Writing

Sometimes it may be necessary to find out if some fraudulent addition has been
made to the existing text. he sequence of strokes is studied to find out the
order of writing. The order of writing may ordinarily be determined only when
the two portions of writing overlap with lines crossing at one or more points.

The following points usually help to determine the sequence:

The ink will spread from the new line to the old line. The following points usually help to determine the sequence:

The ink will spread from the new line to the old line.

The pen may have created a furrow like written line. The upper line may
show continuity while the lower one shows discontinuity.

The fibers along the writing lines get disturbed if the pen nibs are defective.
The disturbance of the fibers may indicate the sequence of strokes.

The ink writing over the folds in a paper may also be readily perceived by
the manner in which the ink runs laterally into the broken paper fibers. No
such effect is apparent when the paper is folded after the writing is made.
When two pencil lines cross, the second line will disturb the graphite marks
of the first, while superimposing the new marks.

Taking the above factors into consideration, the experienced document
expert can determine successfully the order of writing.

Documents should be packed in clean paper/cloth lined/cellophane covers.

Charred documents should be carefully lifted and placed in a suitable card

LATEST PSYCHOLOGICAL TESTS
IN CRIME INVESTIGATION

Introduction

The word ‘polygraph’ denotes graphic representation of multiple/many
parameters under examination. In the present context the parameters under
study are meant for knowing whether a person subjected to the examination is
telling truth or lying. In other words the polygraph test is intended for lie-
detection of the person under examination. That is why the polygraph
equipment is also known as lie-detector and the process is known as Lie-
Detection.

It makes a continuous record of BP, Pulse, respiration and electro dermal
reaction changes in response to stimuli in the form of questions. It makes a continuous record of BP, Pulse, respiration and electro dermal
reaction changes in response to stimuli in the form of questions.

In other words Lie- detection means the detection of deceptive
statements. This instrument only gives us the possibility to verify the veracity
of the statements given by an individual. These statements given by an
individual could be deceptive or truthful. Therefore the result gives a lead
only to the investigating officer, as the report of the test is not valid in the
court of the Law.

Principle of Polygraph

When a person is not in stressful situation than the sympathetic nervous system
and the Parasympathetic nervous system balance each other and maintain body
functions at the normal, stable level called Homeostasis. The polygraph is based on the principle of these psychosomatic interactions
of an individual. This means that psychologically, a change takes place in a
person who consciously holds his feelings, which manifest into physiological
changes in his blood pressure, pulse rate, respiration and electro dermal
response as explained above. In other words a person after telling a lie, gets
emotionally disturbed due to the fear of being detected. This disturbed
feeling gets reflected in the form of physiological changes. Hence it is the
fear of detection and not the guilt which produces measurable physiological
responses. LATEST PSYCHOLOGICAL TESTS IN CRIME INVESTIGATION | 392

Polygraph Equipment

As already mentioned Polygraph records changes in respiration, blood pressure,
pulse and galvanic skin resistance and to measure these:

* One lead is fastened around the chest and another around the abdomen to
measure variations in respiration.

* One blood pressure cuff is attached to the subject’s upper arm.

* Electrodes are attached to index and ring fingers to measure galvanic skin

Legal Status

The results of polygraph examination are not accepted as direct evidence but
only as corroborative evidence, both in criminal and in civil courts in most of
the countries including our country. There is no provision in Cr. P.C., Evidence
Act to accept the results of polygraph. Police have been using it as an aid to
interrogation.

FINGER PRINTS

Introduction

Fingerprints serve as the most valuable, infallible and accurate means of
personal identification, and have since long become an indispensable tool in
the investigation of crime and administration of justice. Investigation of a crime
is primarily meant to identify the person or persons responsible for the crime
and fingerprints provide a scientific clue to the identity of criminals.

The palmer surface of the hands and the plantar surface of the foot are
covered by a layer of corrugated skin to ensure a firmer grip and to resist
slippage. ‘The elevated surfaces are known as friction ridges, while the valleys
formed between two ridges are known as furrows or grooves. The reproduction
of patterns of the friction ridges on the distal phalanges of the fingers and
thumbs are called ‘fingerprints’. The friction ridges are dotted with pore openings meant for discharging
perspiration. Perspiration is largely made of water (98.5%). The remainder
contains organic acids, salts (mainly sodium chloride), urea and at times a very
small quantity of albumin. The definite patterns and ridge details formed by
these fingers (in other words fingerprints) offer a means of identification.

Individuality of Fingerprints

The two fundamental principles of fingerprint science are

1. The ridges formed before birth does not change until destroyed by
decomposition after death.

2. ‘Two fingerprints can be identical only if they are both produced by the
same finger of the same person.

These two principles are applicable to the entire palmer surface of the hands
as well as plantar surface of the feet. FINGER PRINTS | 404

In other words,

¢ A fingerprint is individual and is not shared by any two people. Each person
has his or her own set of fingerprints. Even though identical twins have
the same DNA, they have different fingerprints.

¢ A fingerprint remains unchanged throughout life. The ridge patterns on
fingers never change during the life of an individual. If someone burns
his fingers, the prints disappear for some period but as the wound on the
skin heals, the prints reappear.

* Fingerprint exhibit general patterns that provide a basis for classification.

Classification of Fingerprints

All fingerprints can be divided into four basis types.

1. Arches: In this pattern, the ridges run from one side of the print to
the other. These account for about 5% of fingerprints. 1. Arches: In this pattern, the ridges run from one side of the print to
the other. These account for about 5% of fingerprints.

2. Loops: A loop has delta and a core. A loop is formed by one or
more ridges entering at the one side of the pattern,
continuing up to the centre and recurving around a core to
form a loop. 60 to 65% of all patterns are of loop type.

3. Whorls: It has two deltas and at least one ridge making a complete
circuit. 30 to 35% of all patterns are of whorl types.

4. Compsites: Composites are a combination of any of the above
mentioned three patterns.

Henry System of Ten Digit Classification

Sir Edward Henry, an inspector general of the British police in India’s Bengal
province, worked on a fingerprint classification system for many years. The
Henry System, which he completed in 1899, still is used with certain
modifications. Classification of fingerprints is a method by which a set of
fingerprints may be suitably filed in a filing system and easily retrieved for future
use. The classification formulate consist of letters and numbers arranged in
the form of a fraction with a numerator and denominator.

Collection of Chance Prints from the Scene of Crime

Fingerprints left by the culprit at the scene of crime are known as ‘Chance
prints’, as they are left by chance by the culprit and also called as ‘burglar’s
visiting cards’.

Classes of chance prints

Visible Prints: They occur when a substance such as blood, ink, paint, dirt
or grease on the fingers of perpetrator of a crime leaves
behind a readily visible print. They can be easily recorded
by taking photographs.

Plastic Prints: | These have a three-dimensional quality and occur when the
perpetrator impresses a print into a soft substance such as
wax, putty, candle, soap, mud, dried bloodstains, or even
dust. These can be photographed by angular illumination. FINGER PRINTS | 410

Latent Prints: Latent means hidden. These are invisible and cannot be
seen without special lighting or processing. These prints
are formed by the deposition of the colorless greasy
substance from the palmer surface of the fingers. This
greasy substance is formed out of perspiration, dirt and
oily matter from the face, hair, skin, tools, etc., which is
carried by the fingers.

ANAL TOTAATTLATA

A,

Search for the Latent Prints in Crime Scene ANAL TOTAATTLATA

A,

Search for the Latent Prints in Crime Scene

The first duty of a police officer on arriving at the scene of crime is to instruct
everyone to keep his hands off the articles to be examined for latent prints.
The search for chance prints should be conducted carefully and systematically.
The search should start from the point of entry into the premises, such as
windows, ventilators, chimneys, roof holes where chance prints are most
commonly found. Thereafter the search should extend to all places at the scene
where the culprits moved during the commission of crime, ending at the point
of exit. All the articles suspected to have been disturbed or handled by the
offenders during the commission of offence should be thoroughly examined.

Development of Latent Prints by Advanced Techniques

There are two methods for developing latent fingerprints. They are:
1. Physical Methods
2. Chemical Methods

FINGER PRINTS | 411

Physical Methods

Physical methods are based on the fact that perspiration and greasy matter retain
certain substances. Powder development, iodine fuming are some of the
physical methods for developing latent prints.

Powder Development Methods

& Powder Development Methods

&

Fingerprint powders adhere to moisture and oils of the residue in a latent print
and thereby expose the pattern of the friction ridges. The powders come in a
variety of colors and types. Powders that give the greatest degree of contrast with
the background surfaced are used. Black powder, which is made from carbon
black or charcoal, and grey powder, made from aluminum or titanium powder
issued most often. ‘The powder is applied to a surface by dipping the brush (made
of Camel/Squirrel hair brush or Ostrich feather) and tapping the powder gently
on the developing surface. As powder is brushed over area containing latent print
the particles adhere to the greasy deposits. Once the outlines of the pattern are
seen, the brush movements are made along the flow of ridges. FINGER PRINTS | 412

| = o

Magnetically sensitive powders also are used, and they too come in varying
colors. These powders are used with a special magnetic brush that does not
come into contact with the print, making it less likely to damage or smear the
print. This brush deposits the powder when it is passed over the print and the
print comes into view.

Other specialized powders are fluorescent. After they are applied, the print
fluorescence (glows) observed under Ultra Violet light or Laser.

After the powdering process is complete, the print is either photographed
or lifted. Lifting is done by gently laying the sticky surface of a strip of
transparent tape over the print. As the tape is peeled off, the print pattern sticks
to the tape, which is then placed on a card for later examination and matching.

Chemical Methods

Chemical methods alter the components of perspiration causing a reaction
giving rise to certain colorization e.g., Ninhydrin and silver nitrate. Chemical methods alter the components of perspiration causing a reaction
giving rise to certain colorization e.g., Ninhydrin and silver nitrate.

Ninhydrin:Ninhydrin has been used for years to reveal latent prints. The object
with the supposed latent print is dipped in or sprayed with a ninhydrin solution.
A\s the reaction between the ninhydrin and the oils of the print is extremely
slow, the latent print may take several hours to appear as a purple-blue print.

FINGER PRINTS | 413

ie}
Ninhydrin

Silver Nitrate: Silver nitrate is a component of black-and-white photographic
film. When a latent print is exposed to silver nitrate, the chloride in salt (sodium
chloride) molecules present in the print residue reacts with the silver nitrate
and forms silver chloride. This colorless compound develops, or becomes
visible, when it is exposed to ultra violet light, revealing a black or reddish-
brown print. This method is useful for paper, cardboard and even unpainted
wood. Older fingerprints can be developed by this method.

Silver Nitrate

Cyanoacrylate Vapor: When heated or mixed with sodium hydroxide,
cyanoacrylate releases vapors that bind to amino acids that are present in the
print residues, thus forming a white latent print.

After a print has been exposed with cyanoacrylate, it can be photographed
as it is or treated with a fluorescent dye that binds to the print. The print then
glows under a laser or ultraviolet light. FINGER PRINTS | 414

Iodine fuming method: On heating, solid crystal iodine releases iodine vapors into
a fuming chamber, where iodine fumes combine with oils in the latent print to
produce a brownish print. As this kind of print fades quickly, it must be
photographed immediately. This method is most useful on porous surfaces
such as paper, cardboard etc.

Other Methods: Laser is used for developing latent prints as organic
substances like oils present in the latent print luminescence under laser. These
prints can be photographed using special filters. Very old prints as old as ten
years can be developed using this method on surfaces like plastic, rubber,
painted walls, paper, wood, etc. It is highly sensitive and capable to develop
latent prints that cannot be developed by other methods.

Identification Methods of Finger Prints By Using Latest Technology

Automated Fingerprints Identification System (AFIS)

Fingerprints are widely used as an important tool in crime investigation. The
manual system of fingerprint identification previously used, in fingerprint
Bureau is tedious, complex and time consuming. The enormous increase in
volume of crime coupled with the consequent increase in record has rendered
the manual comparison of fingerprints an unmanageable task. Also if the
chance prints found at the scenes of crime happen to be poor quality, manual
comparison becomes virtually impossible.

Ge Ge

Another serious limitation of the manual identification system is the
comparison of chance prints lifted from the scenes of crime with limited data
of the single digit records maintained at district finger print units and state
finger print bureau. Further, identification of an arrested person and the
identification of chance prints lifted from the scenes of crime have to be done
in the shortest possible time.

Drawbacks with manual searching of Finger Print Records and Necessity for
computerization

Some of the drawbacks are:

1. With the increase in volume of fingerprint records, manual searching of
search references of suspects and accused has become laborious and time
consuming, Therefore it has become difficult to identify the chance prints
developed at the scenes of crime, using the manual system. FINGER PRINTS | 416

2. It used to take number of days before a search slip is identified and result
is communicated to the concerned police.

3. As the record slips are subjected to frequent handling they are liable to wear
and tear and get damaged.

Constant and careful maintenance is required to prevent misfiling.

5. Updating of records becomes a problem as classification formula or seria
number furnished by the police is not always correct.

6. Each incoming fingerprint slip of the convicted and reconvicted criminal
has to be searched against main records to avoid duplication.

7. To avoid all these draw backs in manual searching it became essential to
computerize the Finger Prints records and to introduce computer search
for the purpose of verification of the finger prints slips of suspected
persons and chance prints.

Papillon Automated Finger Print System Papillon Automated Finger Print System

After computerizing the entire state finger print records of the Districts and
State FPB, fingerprint staff are now able to identify the local and inter district
criminals and communicate the search results very rapidly to the IOs within
hours, which was highly impossible under manual systems. After the
introduction of Papillon Automated Finger Print System (AFIS) all manual
verifications were discarded. Now fingerprint Experts are able to identify inter-
district offenders through the chance prints lifted from the crime scenes
through the on-line system.

Theory of AFIS

Automated fingerprints identification system is based on pattern recognition
theory. It involves in:

* Selection of characteristic features from the patterns to be identified.

* Extraction of these features from a set of reference patterns. * Selection of characteristic features from the patterns to be identified.

* Extraction of these features from a set of reference patterns.

* Storing them on a computer to form the database of the known patterns.
* Extraction of similar features from a given pattern to be identified at later date.

* Comparison of these features against those of the known patterns in the
database to decide if a match exists among the later.
